stem: Let \( h(x) = ([[a]]x + ([[b]]))^{[[n]]} \). Using the chain rule, what is \( h'([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Writing \( h(x)=u^{[[n]]} \) with \( u=[[a]]x+([[b]]) \), the chain rule gives \( h'(x)=[[n]]u^{[[n]]-1}\cdot u' = [[n]]([[a]]x+([[b]]))^{[[n]]-1}\cdot [[a]] \). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the chain-rule factor \( u'=[[a]] \) from the outer power rule.
This multiplies by \( [[a]] \) but forgets to lower the exponent by one.
This evaluates \( h([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its derivative.
This lowers neither the exponent nor multiplies by \( u' \), just restates \( n\cdot u^{n} \).
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( h(x) = ([[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]]))^{[[n]]} \). Using the chain rule, what is \( h'([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Writing \( h(x)=u^{[[n]]} \) with \( u=[[a]]x^{2}+([[b]]) \), the chain rule gives \( h'(x)=[[n]]u^{[[n]]-1}\cdot u' = [[n]]([[a]]x^{2}+([[b]]))^{[[n]]-1}\cdot 2\cdot[[a]]x \). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the entire inner-derivative factor \( u'=2\cdot[[a]]x \).
This uses \( u'=[[a]]x \), forgetting the factor of 2 from differentiating \( x^{2} \).
This reports only the inner derivative \( u'=2ax \), forgetting the outer power-rule factor entirely.
This uses \( u'=2\cdot[[a]] \), forgetting to multiply by \( x \).
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( h(x) = \sqrt{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \). Using the chain rule, what is \( h'([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Writing \( h(x)=\sqrt{u} \) with \( u=[[a]]x+([[b]]) \), the chain rule gives \( h'(x)=\dfrac{u'}{2\sqrt{u}}=\dfrac{[[a]]}{2\sqrt{[[a]]x+([[b]])}} \). Since \( [[a]]\cdot[[x0]]+([[b]])=[[k]]^{2} \), evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 in the denominator from the chain rule.
This ignores the inner expression entirely, using only the coefficient \( a \).
This evaluates \( h([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its derivative.
This drops the chain-rule factor of \( [[a]] \) in the numerator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( h(x) = e^{[[a]](x - ([[p]]))} \). Using the chain rule, what is \( h'([[p]]) \)?
solution: Writing \( h(x)=e^{u} \) with \( u=[[a]](x-([[p]])) \), the chain rule gives \( h'(x)=[[a]]e^{u} \). At \( x=[[p]] \), \( u=0 \) so \( e^{u}=1 \), giving \( h'([[p]]) = [[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the chain-rule factor of \( [[a]] \) entirely, reporting \( e^{0} \) alone.
This has the wrong sign on the chain-rule factor.
This evaluates \( e^{[[a]]} \), mistakenly treating the exponent as if \( x-p=1 \).
This multiplies by an extra, unneeded factor of \( e^{[[a]]} \).
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( h(x) = \ln([[a]]x + ([[b]])) \). Using the chain rule, what is \( h'([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Writing \( h(x)=\ln(u) \) with \( u=[[a]]x+([[b]]) \), the chain rule gives \( h'(x)=\dfrac{u'}{u}=\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[a]]x+([[b]])} \). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the inner coefficient as if it were \( u=x \), giving \( 1/a \) instead of \( a/u \).
This reports only the chain-rule factor \( u'=[[a]] \), forgetting to divide by \( u \).
This evaluates \( h([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its derivative.
This divides by \( u+1 \) instead of \( u \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( h(x) = \sin\!\big([[a]](x - ([[p]]))\big) \). Using the chain rule, what is \( h'([[p]]) \)?
solution: Writing \( h(x)=\sin(u) \) with \( u=[[a]](x-([[p]])) \), the chain rule gives \( h'(x)=[[a]]\cos(u) \). At \( x=[[p]] \), \( u=0 \) so \( \cos(u)=1 \), giving \( h'([[p]]) = [[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly evaluates \( \sin(0) \) instead of the derivative \( [[a]]\cos(0) \).
This has the wrong sign on the chain-rule factor.
This evaluates \( \cos([[a]]) \), mistakenly treating the argument as if \( x-p=1 \).
This multiplies by an extra, unneeded factor of \( \cos([[a]]) \).
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
